old_claim.note: Public Key Infrastructure provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Multi-factor authentication can combine passwords, OTP devices, out-of-band methods, and biometrics without using X.509 certificates or a certificate authority.

source_shape.source_locator: Authenticator requirements and authenticator-type sections cover passwords, look-up secrets, out-of-band devices, single- and multi-factor OTP, and cryptographic authenticators.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: NIST defines several MFA-capable authenticator combinations, including password and OTP mechanisms that do not depend on certificate infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: MFA is defined by combining evidence factors, not by a particular key-distribution system; PKI-backed cryptographic authenticators are one option among multiple conforming choices.

ontology_before: The graph made a particular cryptographic trust infrastructure a direct cause of every multiple-factor authentication design.
ontology_after: MFA depends on combining distinct authentication factors and remains compatible with both PKI-backed and non-PKI authenticators.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-pki-independent-identity-and-primitive-boundaries: multi_factor_authentication no longer directly depends on public_key_infrastructure.
would_reject_if: Authoritative MFA requirements redefine multiple-factor authentication so that every conforming combination must use X.509 certificates.
short_reason: NIST recognizes password, OTP, out-of-band, and biometric factor combinations, so certificate infrastructure is not mandatory for MFA.
